What Cal proves

Clarity before dispatch.

Built around the L.E. Isley & Sons Plumbing workflow, Cal demonstrates a reusable AtlasFlow pattern for field service: a messy request becomes AI triage, a structured job record, Operational Viewer visibility, human review, and a clear recommended next action

Product thesis

Service requests arrive messy and inconsistent, forcing dispatchers to interpret urgency, risk, and next steps manually. Cal can turn that unstructured input into clear, prioritized, dispatch-ready work while directing owner attention to the requests that matter most.

Validation question

Do Cal’s core artifacts help L.E. Isley & Sons Plumbing turn messy service requests into faster, more consistent decisions by surfacing urgent or high-risk work sooner and clarifying the right next action—without removing human control?

Build proof

Cal’s working operating layer.

These Airtable views show how Cal turns unstructured service requests into visible, prioritized, dispatch-ready work—while preserving human review and clear ownership.

01 · Morning Brief

Morning Brief

The owner’s daily starting point.

A concise summary of workload, urgent jobs, records needing review, and the recommended focus for the day.

02 · All Service Requests

All Service Requests

One structured operating record.

Cal turns each messy request into a consistent record with customer details, job type, urgency, priority, confidence, an AI summary, and a recommended next action.

03 · Needs Review

Needs Review

Human judgment stays in control.

Yellow-confidence or incomplete requests are separated into a focused queue so the office can review the situation before acting.

04 · Ready for Dispatch

Ready for Dispatch

The next action is clear.

Requests with enough information to move forward are surfaced for scheduling, technician assignment, and dispatch preparation.

05 · Emergency/High Priority

Emergency/High Priority

Risk rises to the top.

Emergency and high-priority work is isolated from the broader queue so active water, sewage, or property risk can receive immediate attention.

06 · RYG Confidence

RYG Confidence

Confidence becomes visible.

Green, Yellow, and Red groupings show where Cal is confident, where a human should review, and where risk requires escalation.

07 · Assigned Tech

Assigned Tech

Ownership is visible before the handoff.

The office can see work grouped by technician, identify unassigned requests, and understand who is carrying each service commitment.

OverdueCustomer callback

Owner focus

Confirm emergency coverage first, resolve Karen Mills’s overdue callback, then review the Mark Reynolds estimate scope before assigning an estimator.
Capacity signal

Morning schedule is nearly full. One same-day slot remains after 2:30 PM.

Requests needing judgment

Mark Reynolds estimate scope and technician parts note both require office review.

Recommended communication
